content:
5 min read
1 hour ago

--

If you’ve ever written the same LIST / GET / POST / PATCH / DELETE boilerplate five times in one sprint, you already know the pain. This post shows how **fastapi-viewsets** erases that boilerplate for a real-world use case: a tiny but fully functional shop admin panel.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The problem with plain FastAPI CRUD
FastAPI is fantastic — but it has no built-in opinion on how to organise CRUD. Most tutorials end up looking like this:
@router.get("/products", response_model=list[ProductSchema])
def list_products(db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
return db.query(Product).all()@router.post("/products", response_model=ProductSchema)
def create_product(body: ProductCreateSchema, db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
obj = Product(**body.dict())
db.add(obj); db.commit(); db.refresh(obj)
return obj
# … repeat for GET, PATCH, DELETE, and then again for Category, Order …
Three resources × five endpoints = 15 nearly identical functions. The moment you need pagination or auth, you add that in 15 places.
fastapi-viewsets brings Django REST Framework–style ViewSets to FastAPI: one class declaration auto-generates all six standard CRUD routes.
What we’re building
A minimal shop admin API with three resources:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Project setup
pip install "fastapi-viewsets[sqlalchemy]" aiosqlite uvicornWe use async SQLAlchemy + aiosqlite for a zero-config local database. In production swap aiosqlite for asyncpg (PostgreSQL) or aiomysql (MySQL) — the rest of the code stays the same.
Create a .env file in the project root:
ORM_TYPE=sqlalchemy
SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URL=sqlite:///./shop.db`fastapi-viewsets` reads this automatically via `python-dotenv`.

Step 1 — Models (`models.py`)
from sqlalchemy import Column, ForeignKey, Integer, Numeric, String, Text
from fastapi_viewsets.db_conf import Baseclass Category(Base):
__tablename__ = "categories"
id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
name = Column(String(128), nullable=False, unique=True)
description = Column(Text, nullable=True)
class Product(Base):
__tablename__ = "products"
id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
name = Column(String(255), nullable=False)
price = Column(Numeric(10, 2), nullable=False)
stock = Column(Integer, default=0, nullable=False)
category_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey("categories.id"), nullable=True)
class Order(Base):
__tablename__ = "orders"
id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
customer_email = Column(String(255), nullable=False)
product_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey("products.id"), nullable=False)
quantity = Column(Integer, default=1, nullable=False)
status = Column(String(32), default="pending", nullable=False)
Plain SQLAlchemy — nothing special here.
Step 2 — Pydantic schemas (`schemas.py`)
from decimal import Decimal
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, EmailStr, Fieldclass CategorySchema(BaseModel):
model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)
id: Optional[int] = None
name: str = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=128)
description: Optional[str] = None
class ProductSchema(BaseModel):
model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)
id: Optional[int] = None
name: str = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=255)
price: Decimal = Field(..., gt=0)
stock: int = Field(default=0, ge=0)
category_id: Optional[int] = None
class OrderSchema(BaseModel):
model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)
id: Optional[int] = None
customer_email: EmailStr
product_id: int
quantity: int = Field(default=1, ge=1)
status: str = Field(default="pending")
`model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)` is the Pydantic v2 equivalent of `orm_mode = True`. It lets the viewset serialize ORM objects directly into the response.
Step 3 — Viewsets (`routers.py`)
This is where the magic happens. Three resources, ~20 lines:
from fastapi.security import OAuth2PasswordBearer
from fastapi_viewsets import AsyncBaseViewset
from fastapi_viewsets.db_conf import get_async_sessionfrom models import Category, Product, Order
from schemas import CategorySchema, ProductSchema, OrderSchema
oauth2 = OAuth2PasswordBearer(tokenUrl="/token")
# --- Categories ---
categories = AsyncBaseViewset(
endpoint="/categories",
model=Category,
response_model=CategorySchema,
db_session=get_async_session,
tags=["Admin / Categories"],
)
categories.register(
methods=["LIST", "GET", "POST", "PATCH", "DELETE"],
oauth_protect=oauth2,
protected_methods=["POST", "PATCH", "DELETE"],
)
# --- Products ---
products = AsyncBaseViewset(
endpoint="/products",
model=Product,
response_model=ProductSchema,
db_session=get_async_session,
tags=["Admin / Products"],
)
products.register(
methods=["LIST", "GET", "POST", "PATCH", "DELETE"],
oauth_protect=oauth2,
protected_methods=["POST", "PATCH", "DELETE"],
)
# --- Orders ---
orders = AsyncBaseViewset(
endpoint="/orders",
model=Order,
response_model=OrderSchema,
db_session=get_async_session,
tags=["Admin / Orders"],
)
orders.register(
methods=["LIST", "GET", "POST", "PATCH", "DELETE"],
oauth_protect=oauth2,
protected_methods=["POST", "PATCH", "DELETE"],
)
`oauth_protect` wires the `OAuth2PasswordBearer` dependency only to `protected_methods`. `GET /products` and `GET /products/{id}` stay public — perfect for a shop storefront.
Step 4 — Main app (`main.py`)
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi_viewsets.db_conf import async_engine, Basefrom routers import categories, products, orders
app = FastAPI(title="Shop Admin API")
@app.on_event("startup")
async def create_tables() -> None:
async with async_engine.begin() as conn:
await conn.run_sync(Base.metadata.create_all)
app.include_router(categories)
app.include_router(products)
app.include_router(orders)
Run it:
uvicorn main:app --reloadOpen http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs — you’ll see a fully populated Swagger UI with all 15 endpoints, grouped by tag, with request/response schemas inferred from your Pydantic models.
Bonus: custom LIST with search and ordering
The built-in `list` gives you `limit` / `offset` pagination out of the box. Need to search products by name and sort by price? Subclass and override `list` — no need to touch the other four methods:
from typing import List, Optional
from sqlalchemy import select
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession
from fastapi_viewsets import AsyncBaseViewsetfrom models import Product
from schemas import ProductSchema
class ProductsViewSet(AsyncBaseViewset):
async def list(
self,
limit: int = 20,
offset: int = 0,
search: Optional[str] = None,
order_by: str = "name",
) -> List[ProductSchema]:
session: AsyncSession = self.db_session()
try:
stmt = select(self.model)
if search:
stmt = stmt.where(self.model.name.ilike(f"%{search}%"))
column_map = {
"name": self.model.name,
"price": self.model.price,
"stock": self.model.stock,
}
col = column_map.get(order_by.lstrip("-"))
if col is not None:
stmt = stmt.order_by(col.desc() if order_by.startswith("-") else col.asc())
stmt = stmt.offset(offset).limit(limit)
rows = (await session.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()
return [ProductSchema.model_validate(row) for row in rows]
finally:
await session.close()
products = ProductsViewSet(
endpoint="/products",
model=Product,
response_model=ProductSchema,
db_session=get_async_session,
tags=["Admin / Products"],
)
products.register(
methods=["LIST", "GET", "POST", "PATCH", "DELETE"],
oauth_protect=oauth2,
protected_methods=["POST", "PATCH", "DELETE"],
)
`GET /products?search=apple&order_by=-price&limit=5` — search by name, sort by price descending, take top 5. FastAPI picks up the new query parameters automatically — no route redeclaration needed.
Eager loading for nested schemas
If `ProductSchema` included a nested `CategorySchema`, you’d normally hit an N+1 problem on `GET /products`. With `fastapi-viewsets` v1.3+ you fix it declaratively — just add an inner `RelatedConfig` to the schema:
class ProductWithCategorySchema(BaseModel):
model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True) id: Optional[int] = None
name: str
price: Decimal
category: Optional[CategorySchema] = None # nested model
class RelatedConfig:
select_related = ["category"] # → one JOIN, no extra queries
Pass this as `response_model` to the viewset and `LIST` / `GET` will automatically apply `joinedload(“category”)` — zero extra configuration.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Wrapping up
`fastapi-viewsets` doesn’t hide FastAPI — it builds on top of `APIRouter`, so every familiar concept (dependencies, middleware, lifespan events) keeps working exactly as you’d expect. The viewset is just the boilerplate layer you shouldn’t have to write by hand every time.
The full source for this example is available on GitHub: [github.com/svalench/fastapi_viewsets](https://github.com/svalench/fastapi_viewsets).
If you found this useful, consider giving the repo a ⭐ — it helps other developers discover it.
